steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The claim states Newland ‘compares his intellect favorably’ to other men. A supporting quotation must show him directly measuring his mind against theirs and coming out ahead.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — "Newland Archer felt himself distinctly the superior of these chosen specimens of old New York gentility; he had probably read more, thought more, and even seen a good deal more of the world, than any other man of the number.": An explicit intellectual comparison with Newland on top. ✓
  • Choice B — "Thinking over a pleasure to come often gave [Newland] a subtler satisfaction than its realisation. This was especially the case when the pleasure was a delicate one, as his pleasures mostly were.": Describes his taste for anticipation, not an intellectual comparison with others. ✗
  • Choice C — "No expense had been spared on the setting, which was acknowledged to be very beautiful even by people who shared [Newland's] acquaintance with the Opera houses of Paris and Vienna.": Describes the opera house; no comparison of minds at all. ✗
  • Choice D — "Singly [the men around Newland] betrayed their inferiority; but grouped together they represented 'New York,' and the habit of masculine solidarity made him accept their doctrine on all the issues called moral.": Touches on social conformity, not on reading, thinking, or intellectual standing. ✗
That is the correct answer.
